title: Disorder-generated multifractals and random matrices: freezing phenomena and extremes
speaker: Yan Fyodorov
abstract: I will start with discussing the relation between a class of disorder-generated multifractals and logarithmically-correlated random fields and processes.

proof_stages.label: Multifractal ansatz and extreme-level question
proof_stages.summary: The talk introduces disorder-generated random intensity patterns on large lattices, defines the singularity spectrum via the density of scaling exponents, and identifies fluctuations of the order-one prefactor as the route to high and extreme values.

proof_stages.label: Exponent calculus from self-similarity and homogeneity
proof_stages.summary: Assuming spatial homogeneity and self-similar two-point power laws for intensity moments, the speaker relates the multifractal spectrum, moment exponents, and correlation exponents by saddle-point/Legendre-transform and consistency arguments.

proof_stages.label: Logarithmic correlations of the multifractal generator
proof_stages.summary: By differentiating the moment and correlation formulae for powers of the field, the centered logarithm of a homogeneous self-similar multifractal field is shown to have logarithmic covariance, while generic non-Gaussianity is emphasized.

proof_stages.label: One-dimensional Gaussian log-correlated model and extreme questions
proof_stages.summary: A regularized 1/f Gaussian Fourier series and its discrete covariance version are presented as tractable log-correlated models whose exponentials form multifractal patterns, leading to questions about the extreme threshold and high-point counts.

proof_stages.label: Random-matrix characteristic polynomials as log-correlated fields
proof_stages.summary: The logarithm of the modulus of CUE and GUE characteristic polynomials is connected, through trace expansions and Gaussian limit theorems, to global and mesoscopic logarithmically correlated processes, including a singular fractional Brownian motion limit with Hurst parameter zero.

proof_stages.label: Statistical-mechanics/freezing framework for extremes
proof_stages.summary: The log-correlated landscape is recast through a Gibbs measure and partition function, and the associated spin-glass/freezing phase transition is proposed as the mechanism that makes extreme-value statistics accessible.

proof_stages.label: Counting-function law and the universal 3/2 correction
proof_stages.summary: Selberg-integral and Fisher-Hartwig moment asymptotics are used to conjecture a power-law distribution for high-point counts, whose typical scale rather than mean gives the universal 2 log M minus 3/2 log log M extreme threshold and its non-Gaussian multifractal analogue.

proof_stages.label: Maximum distribution and applications to CUE and zeta
proof_stages.summary: Using the freezing continuation of partition-function information, the speaker states a conjectural full law for the maximum, compares it with characteristic-polynomial numerics, and translates the prediction to maxima of the Riemann zeta function.

examples.name: Anderson-transition / integer-quantum-Hall wavefunction intensities
examples.note: The Anderson-localization and quantum-Hall wavefunction pictures motivate disorder-generated multifractality and later illustrate why generic multifractal spectra need not be Gaussian/parabolic.
examples.engagement_reason: The Anderson/IQH wavefunction intensities are used as the motivating paradigmatic multifractal pattern, with facts and numerical evidence cited but no concrete calculation on that wavefunction is carried out.

passes.reason: They are critical boundary-state intensities at a localization transition and are later used to show the Gaussian/parabolic-spectrum assumption fails.

examples.name: Maxima of the Riemann zeta function on the critical line
examples.note: The zeta-function discussion is an application of the log-correlated/random-matrix maximum conjecture to number-theoretic data, with the later Q&A spelling out the predicted centering and numerics.
examples.engagement_reason: For the Riemann zeta function the speaker applies the log-correlated maxima picture to intervals high on the critical line, specifies the sampling procedure, and states the resulting correction prediction for the maximum.
